Strategic: The regulatory framing chosen in phase one must sustain institutional momentum through phase three; misalignment between narrative and stakeholder incentives erodes coalition cohesion and reduces probability of procurement-language inclusion from 60–70% to 20–30%.
options: Lead with supplier-concentration and contingency-planning risk, positioning platform neutrality as essential insurance against geopolitical disruption or vendor policy change, and target procurement and risk-management offices rather than political committees.

Anchor the narrative in democratic accountability and public-sector autonomy, arguing that Danish citizens' access to essential services should not depend on foreign commercial platforms' terms of service, and mobilize civil-society and parliamentary allies to drive political pressure.

Frame the issue as interoperability and EU alignment, positioning Denmark's platform-neutral pathway as pioneering implementation of eIDAS2 principles and resilience requirements, and engage EU-level working groups and Nordic peer-countries to amplify legitimacy and reduce perceived parochialism.
levers.review: Controls immediate stakeholder traction vs. long-term political sustainability and coalition durability. Weakness: The options fail to account for how each narrative frames the cost—framing as resilience implies 'insurance cost,' while framing as sovereignty implies 'autonomy investment,' but none explicitly address how procurement budgets are allocated or whether AltID funding can be re-directed toward fallback-pathway development.

levers.name: Demonstrator Validation and External-Review Credibility Architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deciding whether demonstrators undergo formal third-party security review, academic publication, or informal stakeholder presentation determines perceived technical credibility and regulatory acceptability. Systemic: Full third-party review and peer-reviewed publication adds 6–8 weeks and 200–300 KDKK to phase-two costs but increases procurement-language acceptance probability from 40–50% to 70–80%; informal demonstration accepts schedule compression but risks that institutional stakeholders dismiss findings as advocacy-driven or technically unvetted. Strategic: Demonstrator credibility is the primary lever to convert feasibility findings into regulatory requirements; weak external validation permits dismissal as prototype art rather than evidence of viable architecture.
options: Commission an independent security firm and academic cryptography team to conduct formal threat-model analysis and architectural review of both demonstrators, publish findings through peer-reviewed venue, and accept 6–8 week timeline extension and 250 KDKK additional cost for procurement-grade credibility.

Conduct internal security review by project team, document findings in technical report, and circulate to Danish and EU technical working groups for structured feedback without pursuing formal publication or external certification, achieving faster iteration and phase-two agility.

Design demonstrators as open-source reference implementations subject to continuous community review and GitHub-based security audit, positioning the project as transparent-by-design research rather than proprietary proof-of-concept, and invite academic and practitioner participation to build distributed credibility.
levers.review: Controls time-to-credibility vs. cost efficiency and open-source ecosystem alignment. Weakness: The options fail to distinguish between credibility required for phase-three procurement influence (which demands institutional validation) versus credibility required for EU standards bodies (which may accept peer-reviewed publication or open-source stewardship as sufficient).

levers.name: Institutional-Engagement Timing and Gating within Danish Digital Identity Governance
levers.consequences: Immediate: Determining whether to engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, and AltID program office in phase one (exploratory) versus phase two (demonstrator-backed) or phase three (procurement-ready) controls both receptivity and political risk. Systemic: Early phase-one engagement (month 2–3) achieves buy-in and co-design opportunity but risks institutional capture or dilution of findings if operators steer scope toward compatibility rather than independence; late engagement (phase three, month 17+) preserves analytical independence but misses AltID architecture-design gates and faces 'we've already decided' institutional inertia, reducing actual procurement impact by 50–60%. Strategic: The timing window for AltID procurement influence is narrow (likely month 14–18); engagement too early is co-opted, engagement too late is ignored, and the project cannot re-sequence if demonstrator work slips.
options: Initiate structured phase-one engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID program office by month 3, framing as collaborative risk-assessment and early-warning input, and negotiate formal consultation gates at months 6, 12, and 18 to align project outputs with procurement timelines.

Defer formal institutional engagement until phase-two demonstrators are complete and independently reviewed (month 14–15), then present platform-neutral architecture and fallback findings as credible technical evidence that should inform procurement requirements, accepting reduced opportunity for co-design but preserving analytical independence.

Conduct phase-one engagement exclusively with civil-society, academic, and contingency-planning stakeholders (omitting MitID operator and AltID program office), build external coalition strength, and introduce findings to procurement authorities only at phase three via published report and third-party advocacy, maximizing independence but risking late-stage institutional rejection.
levers.review: Controls co-design partnership vs. institutional independence and analytical integrity. Weakness: The options fail to address what happens if AltID procurement timelines slip or if early engagement produces informal feedback that contradicts demonstrator findings—there is no contingency pathway for re-engagement or mid-course correction if institutional landscape shifts.

levers.name: Procurement-Language Insertion and Standards-Harmonization Mechanism
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deciding how the project's fallback-pathway and platform-neutrality findings translate into formal AltID or eIDAS2 procurement requirements determines whether the project influences actual system design or remains influential only in policy discussions. Systemic: Direct insertion into AltID procurement documents (via Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or MitID operator adoption) is highest-impact but faces 95% rejection risk due to incumbent resistance and operational complexity; indirect insertion via EU Digital Identity Wallet materials (eIDAS2 technical standards or Nordic peer alignment) is lower-risk but achieves binding effect only if Denmark implements EU guidance, delaying impact 18–24 months. Strategic: The project's success is measured by procurement-language inclusion; this lever determines whether demonstrators and coalition become regulatory obligation or remain advisory sidebar.
options: Target direct integration into Danish AltID procurement specifications by month 18, negotiating specific requirements for app-store-neutral distribution, platform-independent authentication option, and certified fallback access, and accepting high rejection risk in exchange for maximum direct impact if successful.

Anchor findings in EU Digital Identity Wallet technical specifications and eIDAS2 compliance frameworks, positioning Denmark as early implementer of European resilience and platform-neutrality principles, and accept that binding effect occurs only if and when Denmark formally adopts EU guidance, but achieve broader European legitimacy and peer-country alignment.

Establish platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements in Danish national digital-identity standards (NSIS) and public-sector procurement templates rather than targeting specific AltID procurement, creating systemic pressure for future identity initiatives while avoiding direct confrontation with MitID operators.
levers.review: Controls procurement immediacy vs. European legitimacy and institutional receptivity. Weakness: The options fail to account for procurement-budget availability—even if language is inserted successfully, there is no guarantee that platforms (Apple, Google) will accept app-store terms that comply with platform-neutral or fallback-access requirements, potentially making procurement-language inclusion a symbolic victory with limited operational effect.

levers.name: Coalition Durability and Stakeholder-Alignment Incentive Architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: Designing the coalition's internal governance, decision-making transparency, and communication cadence determines whether civil-society, academic, and policy partners remain engaged through all three phases or defect when political momentum stalls or budget becomes uncertain. Systemic: A tightly coordinated coalition (weekly meetings, shared decision-making, transparent financials) maintains 80–85% stakeholder retention but requires 1.5–2 FTE administrative overhead; a loosely federated model (quarterly meetings, delegated work streams, autonomy) reduces overhead by 60% but risks misalignment and defection when competing priorities emerge, reducing coalition effectiveness from 90% to 50%. Strategic: Coalition collapse at month 12–16 is the most probable project failure mode; phase-three procurement and standards influence is impossible without sustained partner engagement and public credibility.
options: Establish a formal governance structure with monthly steering meetings, transparent budget tracking, shared decision-making on phase-two and phase-three messaging, and written commitment letters from coalition members including contingency-planning provisions if phase-two deliverables slip or institutional engagement falters.

Maintain a distributed coalition with lightweight coordination, delegating phase-two and phase-three work streams to individual partners (academic demonstrators, civil-society advocacy, policy research), minimizing administrative overhead, and reconvene partners only for quarterly reviews and major milestones.

Create a formal multi-stakeholder working group registered with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or a Danish digital-governance body, positioning the coalition as an official advisory entity with defined input into procurement and standards processes, and accept bureaucratic overhead in exchange for institutional legitimacy and formal access to decision-making.
levers.review: Controls administrative efficiency vs. institutional legitimacy and coalition resilience. Weakness: The options fail to specify how the coalition manages conflicting incentives—civil-society partners prioritize open-source and sovereignty arguments, academic partners prioritize publication and research autonomy, and policy partners prioritize procurement access and measurable impact; no governance model eliminates these tensions.

levers.name: Fallback-Implementation Timeline and Risk-Contingency Sequencing
levers.consequences: Immediate: Determining whether fallback-authentication deployment (if procurement is secured) targets immediate integration into AltID (2027–2028) or a secondary wave following AltID stabilization (2029+) affects budget allocation, team sizing, and scope negotiation in phase three. Systemic: Accelerated deployment (2027–2028) requires pre-positioning regulatory approval, vendor partnerships, and citizen-education infrastructure by month 20, adding 1.5–2 million DKK in phase-three contingency costs and accepting 30–40% technical-debt risk; deferred deployment (2029+) preserves quality and reduces phase-three cost by 50% but risks political momentum decay and allows Apple/Google to pre-empt with their own platform-neutral offerings. Strategic: Timeline choice cascades into whether fallback-authentication becomes a primary confidence mechanism for Danish digital identity or remains an insurance product rarely activated, affecting both political support and long-term sovereignty value.
options: Negotiate rapid integration pathway with MitID operator and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, aiming for fallback-authentication availability by 2028 (Q4), prioritizing immediate operational deployment and accepting higher phase-three engineering costs and technical-debt risk in exchange for earliest possible platform-independence achievement.

Plan fallback-authentication deployment for post-AltID stabilization (2029–2030), allowing 12–18 months for citizen adoption, regulatory harmonization, and ecosystem maturation, and use phase-three primarily for standards-language insertion and institutional anchoring rather than production readiness, reducing immediate costs and risk.

Design a phased availability model: minimal fallback option (hardware-token or browser-based authentication without full MitID feature parity) available by 2028, with full-feature and high-assurance fallback following AltID stabilization in 2029–2030, preserving both sovereignty timeline and quality, but increasing phase-three coordination complexity.
levers.review: Controls deployment urgency vs. technical maturity and cost containment. Weakness: The options fail to account for citizen-adoption inertia—even if fallback authentication is technically available, citizens will continue using Apple/Google-mediated MitID unless there is a compelling usability or policy reason to switch, meaning deployment timeline has limited bearing on actual platform-independence unless accompanied by mandatory procurement requirements or citizen-facing incentive design.

Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.
